title: commit
text: commit Commit a transaction to a DBMS or path. commit {"PathString"} Copy Example: commit "DEF" Parameters Table 1. Parameters Parameter Data Type Description PathString String Required path name: If PathString starts with a dollar sign ( $ ), the argument is assumed to be a path name. Modifications to all entities accessed through that path are committed. In this case, assignments determine which entities are committed. If PathString does not start with a dollar sign, the argument is assumed to be a DBMS defined in the modeled entities. Modifications to all entities assigned to that DBMS are committed. In this case, the modeled entity definition determines which entities are committed. If PathString is omitted, all pending updates for all databases used in the transaction are written (committed) and all locked occurrences are unlocked. Return Values Table 2. Values returned in $status Value Meaning 0 The data was successfully committed. -3 Exceptional I/O error (hardware or software). -9 An attempt to open a DBMS failed because the maximum number of DBMS logons has already been reached. -16 Network error: unknown. Table 3. Values commonly returned by $procerror following commit Value Error constant Meaning -2 through -12 <UIOSERR_*> Errors during database I/O. -16 through -30 <UNETERR_*> Errors during network I/O. -1107 <UPROCERR_PATH> The path name is not correct or the path does not exist, for example, no assignment is found for the path. Use Allowed in all component types. Description If a target DBMS does not support database locking, the commit statement is ignored. For information about your DBMS, see the appropriate DBMS Driver Guide. To avoid currency problems, add a commit (or rollback statement) at the highest component level for the current transaction. For example, if Component B is activated by Component A, the commit statement should be in Component A rather than Component B. However, if the component's Component Behavior property is set to Keep component definition and data in memory , you should use commit at that component's level. Example: Using commit The following example shows the use of the commit statement: trigger store call CENSTORE return ($status) end; store function CENSTORE store if ($status < 0) message "Store error!" rollback else message "Store complete." commit if ($status < 0) message "Commit error, rollback performed." rollback endif endif end; CENSTORE Copy Example: Committing DEF versus $DEF Consider the modeled entities with the Model Name property set to MYMODEL . These entities are assigned to the default DBMS. At runtime, assignments are used to redirect the entity FRODO to another DBMS: $DEF = $ORA FRODO.MYMODEL = $SYB Copy If the following commit statement is used, all entities in MYMODEL (which are assigned to the default DBMS) are committed: commit "DEF" Copy If the following commit statement is used, all entities except FRODO are committed: ; commit all entities of MYMODEL except FRODO commit "$DEF" Copy Related Links rollback Undo the transaction (if supported by DBMS). Transaction Control A transaction is a complete, logical unit of work. It usually involves adding, deleting or updating one or more logically related records in one or more databases. With Uniface, you can explicitly define a transaction with the commit ProcScript instruction. If an error occurs, a transaction can be rolled back with the rollback ProcScript instruction.
